front: Countable/co-countable probability measure
back: To verify the countable/co-countable construction, split disjoint families into the cases where all sets are countable or exactly one set is co-countable. Use case: Exercise 1.1.1 and any probability-space verification on unusual sigma-fields.

front: Borel sigma-field from half-open rectangles
back: Show half-open rectangles are Borel, then express rational open rectangles as countable unions of half-open rectangles; use the countable rational base. Use case: Exercises 1.1.2 and 1.1.3; product-measure generation arguments.

front: Increasing union of sigma-fields can fail countable closure
back: Use finite initial-coordinate sigma-fields on N; their union contains every finite set but misses an infinite non-cofinite set such as the evens. Use case: Exercise 1.1.4; distinguishing algebras from sigma-fields.

front: Block construction for asymptotic density counterexamples
back: Use rapidly growing blocks so the latest block dominates all previous blocks; alternate behavior by blocks to destroy the density of intersections. Use case: Exercise 1.1.5 and density/limit counterexamples.

front: Piecewise random variable measurability
back: For Z = X on A and Y on A^c, write {Z <= x} as (A cap {X <= x}) union (A^c cap {Y <= x}). Use case: Exercise 1.2.1; patching random variables on measurable events.

front: Countability of CDF jumps
back: Group jumps by size bigger than 1/m inside bounded intervals; each group is finite because total CDF increase is at most one. Use case: Exercise 1.2.3; monotone-function discontinuity arguments.

front: Monotone density change of variables
back: For Y=g(X) with g increasing, compute F_Y(y)=P(X <= g^{-1}(y)) and differentiate to get f_X(g^{-1}(y))/g'(g^{-1}(y)). Use case: Exercises 1.2.5 and 1.2.6; lognormal and affine density transforms.

front: Density of a square transform
back: For Y=X^2, use F_Y(y)=P(-sqrt(y)<=X<=sqrt(y)) and differentiate to get [f(sqrt(y))+f(-sqrt(y))]/(2sqrt(y)). Use case: Exercise 1.2.7; chi-square with one degree of freedom.

front: Rational cut proof for sum measurability
back: Write {X+Y<x} as the countable union over q in Q of {X<q} cap {Y<x-q}. Use case: Exercise 1.3.2; direct measurability of sums.

front: Lower semicontinuity via closed sublevel sets
back: A function is lower semicontinuous iff all sets {f <= a} are closed; sequential closure proves one direction and contradiction via subsequences proves the other. Use case: Exercise 1.3.5; proving semicontinuous functions are measurable.

front: Discontinuity set from upper and lower local envelopes
back: Define local sup and inf over balls, take limits as radius decreases, and identify discontinuities as the set where the limiting envelopes differ. Use case: Exercise 1.3.6; measurability of discontinuity sets.

front: Constructive representation of sigma(X)-measurable functions
back: Quantize Y into dyadic bins, represent each bin as {X in B}, build Borel simple functions f_n, then take a measurable pointwise limit f with Y=f(X). Use case: Exercises 1.3.8 and 1.3.9; conditional-expectation foundations.

front: Zero integral of nonnegative function implies zero a.e.
back: Use sets {f > 1/n}; each has zero measure because integral dominates (1/n) times its measure, and their union is {f>0}. Use case: Exercise 1.4.1; null-set proofs.

front: Dyadic lower simple approximations
back: Approximate f>=0 by 2^{-n} floor(2^n f); the approximants increase to f and their integrals give monotone sums over dyadic level sets. Use case: Exercise 1.4.2 and simple-function approximation.

front: L1 approximation by continuous functions
back: Approximate an integrable function by simple functions, approximate measurable sets by finite interval unions, then round step-function corners. Use case: Exercise 1.4.3; density of nice functions in L1 on R.

front: Riemann-Lebesgue by step-function reduction
back: Prove oscillatory integral decay for interval indicators explicitly, then approximate any L1 function by a step function. Use case: Exercise 1.4.4; oscillatory integral estimates.

front: Essential supremum bounds integrals
back: If |g| <= ||g||_infty a.e., then |fg| <= ||g||_infty |f| and integration gives the L1-Linfty Holder endpoint. Use case: Exercise 1.5.1; endpoint Holder inequalities.

front: Lp norms converge to Linfty on probability spaces
back: Upper bound by essential supremum; lower bound using sets where |f| exceeds M-epsilon and their positive probability. Use case: Exercise 1.5.2; norm comparisons.

front: Simple functions are dense in Lp
back: Use pointwise simple approximations bounded by |f|, then dominated convergence on |phi_n-f|^p. Use case: Exercise 1.5.9; Lp approximation.

front: Exchange integral and absolutely summable series
back: If sum integral |f_n| is finite, then H=sum |f_n| is integrable; dominated convergence applies to partial sums. Use case: Exercise 1.5.10 and 1.7.1 corollary.

front: Equality case in strict Jensen
back: For strictly convex phi, equality in Jensen forces the random variable to be almost surely constant at its mean. Use case: Exercise 1.6.1; strict convexity equality cases.

front: Two-point distributions as Chebyshev extremizers
back: To prove sharpness of moment/tail inequalities, place small mass at boundary points and the rest at a value chosen to match moments. Use case: Exercises 1.6.3, 1.6.4, and 1.6.5.

front: Cauchy-Schwarz lower bound for positive probability
back: For Y>=0, Cauchy-Schwarz applied to Y*1_{Y>0} yields P(Y>0) >= (EY)^2/EY^2. Use case: Exercise 1.6.6; basic Paley-Zygmund-style estimates.

front: Tail truncation for expectation limits
back: Control small or large tails by splitting the event and using continuity of probability plus elementary bounds on the integrand. Use case: Exercise 1.6.14; limits involving y E(1/X; X>y).

front: Indicator expansion for inclusion-exclusion and Bonferroni
back: Use 1_union = 1 - product_i(1-1_Ai), expand, and take expectations; partial alternating sums give Bonferroni bounds. Use case: Exercises 1.6.9 and 1.6.10.

front: Layer-cake formula via product sets
back: Represent g(x) as the vertical length of {(x,y):0 <= y < g(x)} and apply Tonelli to integrate sections. Use case: Exercise 1.7.2; tail integrals for nonnegative functions.

front: Stieltjes integration by parts with jump correction
back: Interpret Stieltjes integrals as product-measure masses of order regions; the diagonal overlap contributes the product of jump masses. Use case: Exercise 1.7.3.

front: Sliding interval Fubini identity
back: Write F(x+c)-F(x) as mu((x,x+c]), swap integrals, and observe each fixed t is counted for exactly c units of x. Use case: Exercise 1.7.4.

front: Sine integral via Fubini
back: Represent sin(x)/x as an integral of e^{-xy} sin(x), integrate in x first, and bound the remaining exponentially damped tails. Use case: Exercise 1.7.5.

front: Chapter 3.1 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Use Stirling's formula and product-log expansions to turn exact binomial or rare-event probabilities into normal or exponential limits.

front: Chapter 3.2 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Translate weak convergence into CDF continuity-point checks, bounded continuous test functions, open/closed set bounds, tightness, and continuous mappings.

front: Chapter 3.3 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Identify distributions and limits through characteristic functions; use transform tables, Taylor expansions at zero, inversion, and factorization.

front: Chapter 3.4 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Choose the correct normalization, verify Lindeberg or Lyapunov conditions, and reduce random-index or triangular-array sums to known CLT forms.

front: Chapter 3.5 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Use local limit theorem thinking when a problem asks for point probabilities or lattice-scale approximations rather than only distributional convergence.

front: Chapter 3.6 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Prove total variation bounds by coupling, and prove sharpness by constructing a maximal coupling from the common mass of two measures.

front: Chapter 3.7 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Exploit exponential survival functions, Poisson thinning/splitting, and Dirichlet uniform spacings to solve process and occupancy limits.

front: Chapter 3.8 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Read stable-law questions through scaling, regular variation, tail integrals, Laplace transforms, and conditioning on positive stable subordinators.

front: Chapter 3.9 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Use convolution roots, support diameter, symmetrized characteristic functions, and Levy-Khinchin exponent matching for infinite divisibility problems.

front: Chapter 3.10 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Recover marginals by sending coordinates to infinity, build joint laws with copulas, and prove independence or normality by multivariate characteristic functions.

front: Stirling method for binomial ratios
back: Insert Stirling's formula into factorial probabilities, simplify the polynomial prefactor, and exponentiate logarithmic expansions. Use case: Exercises in Section 3.1 involving De Moivre-Laplace or large binomial probabilities.

front: Convert rare-event products to exponentials
back: For products of factors 1+a_{n,k}, take logs and use log(1+x)=x+o(x) when the maximum factor is small. Use case: Birthday, occupancy, no-collision, and geometric waiting-time limits.

front: Linear deviations require exponential-rate estimates
back: When deviations are of order n rather than sqrt(n), normal approximation is usually wrong; use Stirling or large-deviation rates. Use case: Binomial or random-walk tail exercises before the large-deviation chapter.

front: Buffer events for weak convergence
back: Compare events such as {X_n <= x} with {X <= x +/- epsilon} to pass from convergence in probability to weak convergence. Use case: Exercises proving convergence in probability implies distributional convergence or converging-together results.

front: Subsequence principle for identifying limits
back: Show every subsequence has a further subsequence with the same limit; then the original sequence converges. Use case: Tightness and uniqueness arguments in weak convergence exercises.

front: Tightness from tails or coercive moments
back: Control sup_n P(|X_n|>K) directly or use Markov's inequality on a function growing to infinity. Use case: Weak convergence compactness exercises.

front: Converging together and Slutsky template
back: If a main term has a distributional limit and the error goes to zero in probability, use tightness and buffer events to keep the same limit. Use case: Exercises where a statistic is approximated by a simpler statistic.

front: Characteristic-function table matching
back: Recognize transforms such as normal, Cauchy, gamma, Poisson, and uniform; then invoke uniqueness. Use case: Distribution identification and transform inversion exercises in Section 3.3.

front: Extract moments from characteristic-function Taylor terms
back: Differentiate the characteristic function at zero or compare its Taylor expansion with 1+it EX - t^2 E X^2/2 + ... . Use case: Moment and variance calculations from transforms.

front: Independence through characteristic-function factorization
back: A joint characteristic function that factors into marginal characteristic functions identifies the product law. Use case: Exercises proving independence from transforms.

front: Use inversion to recover probabilities or densities
back: Apply the inversion formula in the form appropriate to densities or lattice probabilities, checking integrability when needed. Use case: Characteristic-function inversion exercises.

front: Lindeberg check by truncation
back: Bound the contribution of terms exceeding epsilon times the row standard deviation; bounded or uniformly small terms often make the condition immediate. Use case: Triangular-array CLT exercises.

front: Lyapunov condition as a shortcut to Lindeberg
back: Verify a higher-moment bound divided by the row variance scale; Lyapunov implies Lindeberg. Use case: CLT exercises with available third or higher moments.

front: Random-index CLT template
back: Condition on the random index or compare it to a deterministic equivalent, then use Slutsky once the index ratio converges. Use case: Random sums and stopped-sum CLT exercises.

front: Poisson and multinomial CLT scaling
back: Center counts by their means and scale by square roots of variances; dependence in multinomial vectors appears through covariance constraints. Use case: Occupancy, count-vector, and Poisson approximation CLT exercises.

front: Local limit versus global CLT
back: The CLT controls interval probabilities, while a local limit theorem controls individual lattice probabilities at the normal-density scale. Use case: Point probability approximations in Section 3.5.

front: Check lattice span before using a local limit theorem
back: For lattice variables, the correct local approximation includes the span and only applies on reachable lattice points. Use case: Local normal approximations for sums of integer-valued variables.

front: Any coupling bounds total variation
back: For every event A, |P(X in A)-P(Y in A)| is bounded by P(X != Y), so total variation is no larger than mismatch probability. Use case: Exercise 3.6.1 and coupling estimates.

front: Maximal coupling by common mass
back: Couple two discrete laws by first sampling from their overlap min(mu,nu), then sample residuals on disjoint supports. Use case: Sharp total variation coupling results.

front: Memoryless survival equation
back: The lack-of-memory property gives G(s+t)=G(s)G(t); monotonicity and right-continuity force G(t)=exp(-lambda t). Use case: Exercise 3.7.1 and exponential waiting-time characterization.

front: Competing exponentials race rule
back: The minimum of independent exponentials has rate equal to the sum of rates, and the winning index has probability proportional to its rate. Use case: Exercises 3.7.2 and 3.7.3.

front: Infinite-server queue by Poisson thinning
back: Calls of age r survive with probability 1-G(r); thinning and superposition give a Poisson number in service with mean lambda integral survival. Use case: Exercise 3.7.4.

front: Poisson splitting for occupancy
back: A Poisson number of independent categorized observations splits into independent Poisson counts in each category. Use case: Exercise 3.7.5 and birthday/occupancy computations.

front: Uniform spacings are Dirichlet
back: The gaps between ordered uniform points, including endpoints, are exchangeable Dirichlet(1,...,1) spacings. Use case: Exercises 3.7.7 through 3.7.9.

front: Extreme spacings by first and second moments
back: Count gaps above a threshold, compute one- and two-gap probabilities from simplex volumes, and use variance control. Use case: Largest and smallest uniform spacing limits.

front: No centering for alpha less than one stable limits
back: For alpha<1, small jumps are summable in absolute value under the Levy measure, so the stable limit needs no compensating centering. Use case: Exercises 3.8.1, 3.8.3, and 3.8.5.

front: Borderline normal attraction has sqrt(n log n) scale
back: A symmetric variable with tail P(|Z|>x) like x^{-2} has infinite variance but still lies in the normal domain with a logarithmic variance correction. Use case: Exercise 3.8.2 with p=1/2.

front: Stable fractional moment and tail test
back: Use the integral representation of E|X|^p through 1-Re phi(t) to get finiteness for p<alpha and tail lower bounds to show E|X|^alpha diverges. Use case: Exercise 3.8.4.

front: Positive stable Laplace functional equation
back: Stability gives psi(lambda)^n=psi(n^{1/alpha}lambda); continuity turns this into psi(lambda)=exp(-c lambda^alpha). Use case: Exercise 3.8.6.

front: Stable subordination product rule
back: Condition on a positive beta-stable Y: E exp(it X Y^{1/alpha}) becomes the Laplace transform of Y at c|t|^alpha, giving index alpha beta. Use case: Exercise 3.8.7 and normal-ratio Cauchy derivations.

front: Gamma infinite divisibility by shape splitting
back: A Gamma(a,lambda) characteristic function is the nth power of the Gamma(a/n,lambda) characteristic function. Use case: Exercise 3.9.1.

front: Bounded infinitely divisible laws are degenerate
back: If a bounded law is an n-fold convolution, the summand support diameter is at most 1/n of the total diameter, forcing variance to zero. Use case: Exercise 3.9.2.

front: Infinitely divisible characteristic functions do not vanish
back: Symmetrize to |phi|^2, take nth convolution roots, and use continuity near zero to contradict a zero at a fixed point. Use case: Exercise 3.9.3.

front: Find Levy measures by matching exponents
back: Compare the log characteristic function with the Levy-Khinchin integral; symmetry cancels imaginary compensation terms. Use case: Exercise 3.9.4.

front: Recover marginals from a joint CDF
back: Send all non-target coordinates to infinity and use continuity from below to recover the target coordinate's distribution function. Use case: Exercise 3.10.1.

front: FGM-type copula density check
back: For C(u)=prod u_i + alpha prod u_i(1-u_i), the mixed derivative is 1+alpha prod(1-2u_i), nonnegative for |alpha|<=1. Use case: Exercise 3.10.2.

front: Mixed partial derivative recovers joint density
back: Write the CDF as an iterated integral of the density and apply the fundamental theorem of calculus successively. Use case: Exercise 3.10.3.

front: Joint characteristic function factorization
back: Independence gives product transforms; conversely, a product joint characteristic function matches the product law by uniqueness. Use case: Exercise 3.10.6.

front: Diagonal covariance gives independent normal coordinates
back: For a multivariate normal vector, a diagonal covariance matrix makes the characteristic function factor into one-dimensional normal transforms. Use case: Exercise 3.10.7.

front: Identify multivariate normal laws by all linear combinations
back: If every t dot X is normal with the right mean and variance, then the joint characteristic function is the multivariate normal transform. Use case: Exercise 3.10.8.

front: Chapter 4.1 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Compute conditional expectations by testing against the conditioning sigma-field, then use projection, Jensen, conditioning on partitions, and variance decomposition to simplify identities.

front: Chapter 4.2 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Check martingale type by conditioning one step ahead; use predictable transforms, stopped processes, upcrossing counts, and product decompositions to prove convergence or construct examples.

front: Chapter 4.3 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Use concrete martingale examples to isolate hypotheses: likelihood-ratio processes give martingales, additive error bounds give convergence, and branching recursions reduce to fixed-point equations.

front: Chapter 4.4 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Apply optional sampling and Doob inequalities to stopped martingales; combine orthogonal increments, quadratic martingales, and Kronecker normalization for L2 and strong-law estimates.

front: Chapter 4.6 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Recognize when convergence is upgraded by uniform integrability; use Levy upward and zero-one laws to turn conditioning on growing sigma-fields into posterior consistency or absorbing-state conclusions.

front: Chapter 4.7 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Use backward martingale convergence for tail-like sigma-fields, then exploit exchangeability to replace conditional laws by finite-population hypergeometric formulas.

front: Chapter 4.8 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Choose a martingale matched to the target functional, stop it at bounded levels, justify optional stopping, and pass limits to obtain hitting probabilities, Wald identities, and ruin bounds.

front: Chapter 4.9 method: what is the main exercise strategy?
back: Encode first-return decompositions as renewal equations and solve them with generating functions; singular behavior at one reveals recurrence and return-time means.

front: Conditional Bayes rule from indicator tests
back: To identify E[X | H] on a partition or sub-sigma-field, multiply by indicators of conditioning events and match integrals. Use case: Section 4.1 exercises involving conditional probabilities, finite partitions, and conditional densities.

front: Transfer pointwise inequalities through conditioning
back: If an inequality holds after multiplying by every nonnegative H-measurable test function, it holds for the conditional expectations. Use case: Conditional Jensen, conditional Markov bounds, and monotonicity arguments in Section 4.1.

front: L2 projection Pythagorean identity for conditioning
back: Use E[(X-E[X|H])Z]=0 for H-measurable Z to split squared errors and prove best-approximation identities. Use case: Projection and regression-style conditional expectation exercises.

front: Random-sum variance by conditioning on the count
back: Condition on N to compute E[S_N | N] and Var(S_N | N), then add conditional variance plus variance of the conditional mean. Use case: Random sums and total-variance exercises in Section 4.1.

front: Conditional expectation can preserve distribution only under rigidity
back: If E[X|H] has the same distribution as X and Jensen is tight for a strictly convex function, then X must already be H-measurable. Use case: Exercises where conditioning appears not to reduce randomness.

front: One-step martingale check in the natural filtration
back: For processes built from independent increments, reduce E[X_{n+1}|F_n] to a deterministic function of the current state and one new increment. Use case: Section 4.2 martingale, submartingale, and supermartingale verification.

front: Deterministic martingale-type checks reduce to monotonicity
back: A deterministic adapted process is a submartingale or supermartingale exactly when the underlying sequence is monotone in the corresponding direction. Use case: Exercises testing definitions on simple deterministic examples.

front: Localize upcrossing arguments by stopping at bounded levels
back: Stop a process before it leaves a bounded range so optional inequalities apply, then let the stopping level increase. Use case: Upcrossing and convergence exercises where integrability or boundedness needs to be restored.

front: Product martingales may converge to zero by logarithms
back: For products of independent mean-one factors, examine log factors; a negative accumulated log drift can force almost-sure convergence to zero. Use case: Multiplicative martingales and examples where mean is not preserved at the limit.

front: Dubins-style switching creates many upcrossings
back: Alternate between betting strategies active below one level and above another to convert repeated upcrossings into predictable-transform gains. Use case: Exercises connecting upcrossing inequalities with gambling interpretations.

front: Bounded increments alone do not make a martingale
back: Construct an adapted process with bounded steps but nonzero conditional drift to show martingale conclusions need a true drift condition. Use case: Section 4.3 exercises checking the exact hypotheses of martingale theorems.

front: Summable additive errors preserve convergence
back: Add the tail sum of the error sequence to a near-supermartingale to obtain a genuine supermartingale with the same limiting behavior. Use case: Almost-supermartingale convergence arguments.

front: Partition likelihood ratios form martingales
back: On a refining finite partition, the ratio Q(A)/P(A) on the cell containing omega is the conditional expectation of dQ/dP. Use case: Radon-Nikodym and likelihood-ratio martingale exercises.

front: Bernoulli product likelihood ratios and Hellinger sums
back: For product Bernoulli laws, square-root likelihood overlaps factor, and convergence or singularity is controlled by a summable squared parameter distance. Use case: Exercises comparing infinite product measures.

front: Branching extinction probabilities are fixed points
back: Condition on the first generation to get q = f(q), where f is the offspring generating function; choose the minimal fixed point in [0,1]. Use case: Branching-process extinction and survival exercises.

front: Optional sampling between two stopping times
back: Apply optional sampling to stopped processes at bounded approximations of S and T, then pass to limits using the available integrability control. Use case: Section 4.4 exercises with E[M_T | F_S] and S <= T.

front: Doob L1 logarithmic maximal bound
back: Integrate the weak L1 maximal inequality over levels and split at a scale where the integral of min(1, X/lambda) is finite. Use case: Maximal estimates for nonnegative submartingales without Lp control for p>1.

front: Quadratic martingale exit bounds
back: For a martingale with controlled conditional variances, stop M_n^2 minus accumulated variance at the exit time and compare with the boundary size. Use case: Exit probability and maximal L2 exercises.

front: Martingale increments are orthogonal in L2
back: For i<j, E[D_iD_j]=E[D_i E[D_j|F_{j-1}]]=0, so variances of martingale sums add. Use case: L2 convergence, square-function, and maximal inequality exercises.

front: Kronecker normalization for martingale sums
back: Show a weighted martingale series converges in L2 or a.s., then apply Kronecker's lemma to get normalized partial-sum convergence. Use case: Martingale strong laws and weighted-average exercises.

front: Posterior consistency from Levy upward theorem
back: Write a posterior probability as E[1_A | F_n]; as observations accumulate and generate A, Levy upward convergence sends it to 1_A. Use case: Bayesian consistency and statistical identification exercises.

front: Dyadic conditional expectations approximate the full variable
back: Condition on increasingly fine dyadic sigma-fields; the generated sigma-field is the Borel information, so Levy upward gives convergence. Use case: Exercises approximating a variable by finite-information conditional expectations.

front: Levy zero-one law forces absorption or escape events
back: If conditional probabilities of a tail event stay bounded away from the event indicator, Levy's zero-one law gives a contradiction. Use case: Random-walk, branching, or Markov-chain exercises with eventual absorption events.

front: Bounded martingales with binary absorbing limits
back: A bounded martingale converges a.s.; if the dynamics force every limit to be 0 or 1, the starting value is the probability of limiting at 1. Use case: Urn, posterior, and absorbing-state martingale exercises.

front: Backward martingale Lp convergence by uniform integrability
back: For p>1, boundedness in Lp gives uniform integrability, so backward martingale convergence improves to L1 and often Lp convergence. Use case: Backward conditional expectation exercises in Section 4.7.

front: Pass limits inside backward conditional expectations carefully
back: Use domination or uniform integrability before interchanging conditional expectation limits with ordinary limits. Use case: Backward martingale convergence exercises involving approximating functions.

front: Exchangeability turns conditioning into hypergeometric sampling
back: Given the unordered multiset or the total number of successes, exchangeability makes a fixed subcollection have the finite-population hypergeometric law. Use case: Exchangeable Bernoulli and finite-sample conditional probability exercises.

front: Exchangeable pair covariance constraints
back: Use Var(sum X_i) >= 0 to derive lower bounds on pair covariances and identify the limiting covariance structure. Use case: Exchangeability exercises about correlations and de Finetti-style limits.

front: U-statistic square limits by pair classification
back: Expand the square of the U-statistic and classify pairs by overlap pattern; only non-negligible classes contribute in the limit. Use case: U-statistic convergence exercises under exchangeability or iid assumptions.

front: Optional stopping with a conditional target
back: Stop at T wedge n, prove uniform integrability or boundedness, and pass to E[M_T | F_S]=M_S. Use case: Random-walk and stopping-time exercises with conditional versions of optional stopping.

front: Maximal bounds for nonnegative supermartingales
back: Stop at the first crossing of a level and use the supermartingale property to bound the crossing probability by the initial mean divided by the level. Use case: Ruin, hitting, and overshoot probability estimates.

front: Wald's second equation from a stopped square martingale
back: For centered iid increments, S_n^2 - n sigma^2 is a martingale; optional stopping gives E[S_T^2]=sigma^2 E[T] when justified. Use case: Expected duration and variance identities for stopped random walks.

front: Generate gambler's ruin moments from martingales
back: Use S_n, S_n^2-n, and higher polynomial martingales stopped at ruin boundaries to solve for hitting probabilities and time moments. Use case: Gambler's ruin expected time and variance exercises.

front: Exponential martingale ruin adjustment
back: Find theta with E exp(theta X)=1, stop exp(theta S_n) at a hitting time, and use boundary values to estimate ruin probabilities. Use case: Insurance risk, random-walk ruin, and Lundberg-type inequalities.

front: Fourth-moment martingales bound exit-time moments
back: Construct a polynomial martingale involving S_n^4, S_n^2, and n; stopping it at an exit time produces second-moment bounds for T. Use case: Random-walk exit-time variance and higher-moment exercises.

front: Return-time renewal equations via generating functions
back: Decompose visits into first return plus future visits, obtaining U(s)=1/(1-F(s)); analyze F(s) near s=1 to read recurrence and expected return time. Use case: Section 4.9 random-walk return-time exercise.

front: State sufficiency test for the Markov property
back: A proposed process is Markov only if the retained state contains all information needed to compute the next-step law; record maxima fail when the current walk location is omitted. Use case: Exercises 5.1.1, 5.1.2, 5.1.3, and other state-selection problems.

front: Compute transitions from the one-step mechanism
back: Given the present state, enumerate the next independent draw, swap, birth, or arrival and translate the possible outcomes into transition probabilities. Use case: Coupon, pair-chain, Bernoulli-Laplace, Wright-Fisher, and queue transition computations.

front: Bayesian predictive state for hidden-parameter chains
back: When a hidden parameter is shared across trials, update its posterior from a sufficient statistic and use the posterior mean as the next-step predictive probability. Use case: Exercise 5.1.6 and temporally inhomogeneous Markov chains from exchangeable coin flips.

front: Mendelian pair transition matrix
back: For unordered genotype-pair states, compute one-offspring genotype probabilities (p,q,r) and map two independent offspring to probabilities p^2, 2pq, 2pr, q^2, 2qr, r^2. Use case: Brother-sister mating transition and absorption computations.

front: Past and future conditional independence
back: For A in the past and B in the future, condition first on the present sigma-field; the Markov property turns E(1_B|F_n) into a function of X_n. Use case: Exercise 5.2.1 and conditional independence arguments.

front: First entrance decomposition
back: Partition {X_n=y} by the first positive hitting time T_y=m, then restart from y by the strong Markov property to get p^n(x,y)=sum_m P_x(T_y=m)p^{n-m}(y,y). Use case: Exercise 5.2.4 and renewal decompositions for Markov chains.

front: Exit probabilities solve the Dirichlet problem
back: An exit probability h(x)=P_x(V_A<V_B) is harmonic off A union B with boundary values 1 and 0; optional stopping at the exit time proves uniqueness on finite domains. Use case: Exercises 5.2.7 and 5.2.8.

front: Expected hitting times solve a Poisson equation
back: For g(x)=E_x V_A, first-step analysis gives g=1+Pg off A and g=0 on A; the process g(X_{n∧V_A})+n∧V_A is the uniqueness martingale. Use case: Exercises 5.2.11, 5.2.12, and 5.2.13.

front: Absorption probability from martingale value
back: If a bounded martingale is stopped on two absorbing boundary values, the starting value equals the boundary-weighted absorption probability. Use case: Exercises 5.2.8, 5.2.9, and 5.2.10.

front: Iid excursions between recurrent returns
back: At successive return times to a recurrent state, the strong Markov property makes the interarrival times and path excursions iid. Use case: Exercise 5.3.1 and regenerative proofs in Sections 5.5 and 5.6.

front: Birth-death recurrence by log product asymptotics
back: Use log(q_j/p_j) expansions to decide divergence of the birth-death scale sum; for p_j=1/2+C/j the threshold is C=1/4. Use case: Exercise 5.3.4 and nearest-neighbor recurrence boundaries.

front: Lyapunov function tending to zero proves transience
back: A positive superharmonic function outside a finite set that tends to zero at infinity contradicts recurrent return to that finite set, hence implies transience in irreducible chains. Use case: Exercise 5.3.5 and outward-drift birth-death chains.

front: Power Lyapunov functions for near-critical birth-death chains
back: For p_j-1/2~C/j, Taylor expand E_j X_1^alpha-j^alpha; choosing alpha on the correct side of 1-4C gives recurrence or transience. Use case: Exercise 5.3.6.

front: Recurrent irreducible chains have only constant nonnegative superharmonic functions
back: Stop a nonnegative supermartingale f(X_n) at the hitting time of another state; recurrence forces f(x)>=f(y) and symmetry gives equality. Use case: Exercise 5.3.7 and Liouville-type arguments.

front: Four alternatives for one-dimensional random walks
back: Tail zero-one laws and ladder behavior force a one-dimensional random walk to be identically zero, drift to +infinity, drift to -infinity, or oscillate unboundedly both ways. Use case: Exercise 5.4.1.

front: Ascending ladder epochs regenerate record highs
back: Successive strict ascending ladder epochs repeat the same trial by the strong Markov property; if the first ladder time is defective, only finitely many records occur, otherwise records go to infinity. Use case: Exercise 5.4.2.

front: Recurrent random walk implies nonnegative superharmonic Liouville
back: For recurrent walks in dimensions one and two, f(S_n) is a nonnegative supermartingale and recurrent visits to neighborhoods force all superharmonic values to agree. Use case: Exercise 5.4.3.

front: Bounded harmonic functions via tail zero-one law
back: For a bounded harmonic h, h(S_n) is a bounded martingale; its limit is invariant under finite permutations of iid increments, so Hewitt-Savage forces constancy. Use case: Exercise 5.4.4.

front: Hypergeometric stationary law for Bernoulli-Laplace
back: The Bernoulli-Laplace diffusion has stationary distribution proportional to binomial choices of black and white balls in the left urn; adjacent detailed balance verifies it. Use case: Exercise 5.5.1.

front: Cycle occupation measure as a hitting-probability ratio
back: For recurrent x and y in the same class, expected visits to y during one x-cycle equal P_x(T_y<T_x)/P_y(T_x<T_y). Use case: Exercise 5.5.2.

front: Use normalization to compare stationary measures
back: In an irreducible recurrent chain, all stationary measures are scalar multiples; evaluate at one state to determine the scalar. Use case: Exercises 5.5.3 and 5.5.5.

front: Poisson thinning gives M/M/infinity stationarity
back: If X is Poisson(theta), Bernoulli thinning with survival p gives Poisson(p theta), and adding independent Poisson(lambda) arrivals gives Poisson(p theta+lambda). Use case: Exercise 5.5.8.

front: Idle fraction from reflected random-walk regulator
back: For a stable reflected walk, the regulator increments exactly when the queue is empty and the raw increment is -1; dividing the reflection identity by n gives the long-run idle frequency. Use case: Exercise 5.5.7 and M/G/1 stationary mass at zero.

front: Two-state chain convergence via affine recursion
back: Track one coordinate probability q_n; the recursion q_{n+1}=beta+(1-alpha-beta)q_n solves explicitly around its stationary fixed point. Use case: Exercise 5.6.1.

front: Finite irreducible aperiodic chains have a positive matrix power
back: Use a path from x to y plus sufficiently long return loops at y; finiteness lets one choose a common power m for all pairs. Use case: Exercise 5.6.2.

front: Uniform block coupling gives exponential convergence
back: If an m-step transition has a uniformly positive chance to send all states to one common state, coupling attempts in independent blocks have geometric tails. Use case: Exercise 5.6.3 and finite-state mixing estimates.

front: Regenerative LLN for additive functionals
back: Split the path into iid return cycles; reward sums divided by elapsed time converge to expected cycle reward divided by expected cycle length, which equals the stationary average. Use case: Exercise 5.6.5.

front: Regenerative CLT for additive functionals
back: Apply the iid CLT to centered cycle rewards and random-index by the number of completed cycles; show incomplete-cycle rewards are negligible on sqrt(n) scale. Use case: Exercise 5.6.6.

front: Ratio limits from recurrent cycles
back: Count visits to z per completed y-cycle and divide by visits to y; the strong law gives the stationary-measure ratio m(z)/m(y). Use case: Exercises 5.6.7 and 5.6.8.

front: Split-chain recurrence is return-sum divergence at the atom
back: For the artificial atom alpha, the countable-state return criterion applies: recurrence is equivalent to divergence of sum_n pbar^n(alpha,alpha). Use case: Exercise 5.8.1.

front: Stationary distribution forces Harris recurrence
back: Lift a stationary distribution to the split chain; positive stationary mass at alpha forces an infinite expected number of alpha visits, hence recurrence. Use case: Exercise 5.8.3.

front: Harris recurrence does not depend on the small-set pair
back: If another Harris set A' is reachable, it has positive lambda measure for the original split chain; Theorem 5.8.8 makes visits to A' occur infinitely often. Use case: Exercise 5.8.4.

front: GI/G/1 drift classification
back: Compare the reflected workload with the raw random walk until the first negative passage: positive drift gives transience, nonpositive drift gives recurrence, negative drift gives positive recurrence. Use case: Exercise 5.8.5.

front: Reflected random walk equals reversed maximum in law
back: The reflected workload satisfies W_n=S_n-min_{j<=n}S_j, which by reversing increments has the same law as the maximum of a length-n random walk. Use case: Exercise 5.8.6.

front: Multiplicative variance O.U. becomes stable on log scale
back: For Y_{n+1}=beta sqrt(|Y_n|) Z_{n+1}, log|Y_n| follows an AR(1) recursion with coefficient 1/2, yielding a nonzero positive recurrent Harris class plus an absorbing zero. Use case: Exercise 5.8.7.

front: Invariant sigma-field and invariant random variables
back: Show invariant events form a sigma-field by pullback closure; prove an I-measurable random variable is invariant by checking rational cuts, and conversely use invariant inverse images. Use case: Exercise 6.1.1; identifying E(X|I) and invariant limits.

front: Strict representative for almost invariant sets
back: From an almost invariant set, form a forward pullback union B and then C=intersection phi^{-n}B; C is strictly invariant and differs only by a null set. Use case: Exercise 6.1.2; replacing mod-null invariance by exact invariance.

front: Direct density proof of irrational rotation ergodicity
back: Use pigeonhole gaps to prove the irrational orbit is dense, approximate positive-measure sets by high-density intervals, and contradict coexistence of invariant A and A^c with positive measure. Use case: Exercise 6.1.3; proving ergodicity without Fourier series.

front: Two-sided extension of a stationary sequence
back: Define finite-dimensional laws on integer-indexed coordinates by shifting all indices into the nonnegative side, then use stationarity for consistency and Kolmogorov extension. Use case: Exercise 6.1.4; return-time and cycle arguments needing negative times.

front: Stationarity and ergodicity pass to factors
back: For Y_k=g(X_k,X_{k+1},...), stationarity follows from shifted future laws; invariant events for Y pull back to invariant events for X, so ergodicity passes to Y. Use case: Exercise 6.1.5; creating stationary derived processes.

front: Random phase makes iid blocks stationary and ergodic
back: Concatenate iid blocks and start at an independent uniform phase; one-step shifts rotate the phase and occasionally shift the iid block sequence, giving stationarity and ergodicity. Use case: Exercise 6.1.6; block constructions with stationary output.

front: Gauss map invariant density by branch telescoping
back: For phi(x)=1/x-floor(1/x), split into branches x=1/(k+y); the transformed density sum telescopes to 1/(1+y). Use case: Exercise 6.1.7; verifying the continued-fraction invariant measure.

front: Lp upgrade of Birkhoff by truncation
back: Prove Lp convergence for bounded observables by bounded convergence, then truncate X and control the tail using Jensen plus Lp contraction of conditional expectation. Use case: Exercise 6.2.1; upgrading ergodic averages from L1 to Lp.

front: Moving observable ergodic average
back: Decompose averages of g_m(phi^m) into the fixed g average plus errors; control a.s. errors by tail suprema and L1 errors by Cesaro means. Use case: Exercise 6.2.2; triangular or time-varying observables in ergodic averages.

front: Wiener maximal inequality from maximal ergodic lemma
back: Apply the maximal ergodic lemma to Y=X-alpha so the positivity event of the shifted partial sums is exactly {max S_j/j > alpha}. Use case: Exercise 6.2.3; deriving maximal probability bounds for ergodic averages.

front: Range expectation by last-visit decomposition
back: Count each visited site by its last visit time; stationarity makes the probability of no future revisit over n-m steps equal to g_{n-m}. Use case: Exercise 6.3.1; expected range identities for stationary-increment walks.

front: Positive drift escape probability from range growth
back: With ergodic integer increments bounded above by one and positive mean, the range grows at the same rate as the running maximum; Theorem 6.3.1 identifies the rate with P(escape). Use case: Exercise 6.3.2; recurrence/escape probabilities under stationary increments.

front: Cycle trick occupation ratio
back: Use a two-sided stationary version and partition by the last visit to A before time 0; expected B-occupation in an A-cycle equals P(X0 in B)/P(X0 in A). Use case: Exercise 6.3.3; constructing stationary measures from excursions.

front: Stationary renewal first-waiting bias
back: Under conditioning on a renewal at time 0, stationarity of return gaps gives P(T=n)=Pbar(T>=n)/Ebar T for the waiting time seen from a stationary time. Use case: Exercise 6.3.4; inspection-paradox and stationary renewal calculations.

front: Arbitrarily slow deterministic subadditive convergence
back: Set X_{m,m+k}=f(k) with f(k)/k decreasing; subadditivity follows by comparing both pieces to f(n)/n, so the expectation convergence can be as slow as f(n)/n. Use case: Exercise 6.5.1; understanding limits of Fekete-type convergence rates.

front: Longest common subsequence first-moment bounds
back: Compute small-n expectations for lower bounds and bound P(L_n >= K) by the expected number of matching index-pair subsequences, binom(n,K)^2 2^{-K}. Use case: Exercise 6.5.2; bounding LCS constants.

front: Poisson greedy path lower bound
back: Choose successive Poisson points minimizing x+y in the northeast quadrant; the increment sum has tail exp(-t^2/2), giving mean coordinate step sqrt(pi/8) and gamma >= sqrt(8/pi). Use case: Exercise 6.5.3; lower bounds for Hammersley's increasing path constant.

front: Permutation LIS upper bound by first moment
back: For a fixed k-subset of indices, the values are increasing with probability 1/k!, so E J_k^n=binom(n,k)/k!; Stirling with k~alpha sqrt(n) gives gamma <= e. Use case: Exercise 6.5.4; upper bounds for longest increasing subsequences.

front: Branching Laplace martingale speed bound
back: Normalize the generation-n Laplace sum by (mu phi(theta))^n to get a nonnegative martingale; Markov's inequality bounds early birth events when exp(-theta a)/(mu phi(theta))>1. Use case: Exercise 6.5.5; age-dependent branching speed upper-tail bounds.

front: Two-time Brownian sign probability by Gaussian wedge angle
back: Write B_t as B_s plus an independent increment, standardize to a rotationally symmetric bivariate normal, and compute the sector angle cut out by the two half-planes. Use case: Exercise 7.1.1; probabilities involving signs of correlated Brownian values.

front: Brownian polynomial moments by independent increment expansion
back: Represent B_1, B_2, B_3 through independent unit Gaussian increments, expand the polynomial, and discard terms with centered odd factors. Use case: Exercise 7.1.2; low-order Brownian moment computations.

front: Deterministic Brownian integrals are Gaussian
back: Approximate an integral of B_s against deterministic ds by Riemann sums; each sum is Gaussian, and the variance is the double integral of r wedge s. Use case: Exercise 7.1.3; integrated Brownian motion and Gaussian linear functionals.

front: Events in raw path sigma-field depend on countably many coordinates
back: Show the class of events depending on a countable coordinate list is a sigma-field containing all finite-dimensional cylinders. Use case: Exercise 7.1.4; path-space measurability and cylinder sigma-fields.

front: K-increment obstruction to Brownian Holder regularity
back: If a Holder point exists, a nearby block of k Gaussian increments must all be unusually small; union bound gives probability O(n^{1+k(1/2-gamma)}). Use case: Exercise 7.1.5; proving no Holder points above exponent 1/2.

front: Dyadic quadratic variation by L2 plus Borel-Cantelli
back: Compute variance of the dyadic squared-increment sum as 2t^2 2^{-n}, apply Chebyshev, and sum over n to get almost sure convergence. Use case: Exercise 7.1.6; Brownian quadratic variation along dyadic partitions.

front: Deterministic-time Markov conditioning for hitting survival
back: Condition on B_s=y, replace the future event by a hitting probability for Brownian motion started at y, and integrate against the transition density. Use case: Exercises 7.2.1 and 7.2.2; return-time and last-zero decompositions.

front: Last-zero event as no future hit after conditioning
back: The event L <= t is equivalent to no zero in (t,1]; after conditioning on B_t, this becomes P_y(T_0 > 1-t). Use case: Exercise 7.2.2 and arcsine-law calculations.

front: Dense local maxima from immediate two-sided crossing
back: Take shrinking deterministic intervals, use continuity to get a maximum, and use immediate crossing forward and backward to rule out endpoints. Use case: Exercise 7.2.3; path-local extremum arguments.

front: Germ-field limsup constants via Blumenthal 0-1
back: For a small-time limsup L, events {L <= r} lie in the germ sigma-field; 0-1 probabilities force L to be almost surely constant. Use case: Exercise 7.2.4(i); local Brownian growth rates.

front: Brownian paths are not 1/2-Holder at a fixed time
back: Use B_delta/sqrt(delta) standard normal to get positive probability of arbitrarily large normalized values, then apply Blumenthal 0-1. Use case: Exercise 7.2.4(ii); critical Brownian Holder exponent.

front: F-sigma hitting times as infima of closed hitting times
back: Write the F-sigma set as a countable union of closed sets, use closed-set hitting times, and express {inf T_n < t} as a countable union. Use case: Exercise 7.3.1; proving first-entry times are stopping times.

front: Sum of stopping times via rational split
back: Write {S+T<t} as the union over rational q<t of {S<q} cap {T<t-q}. Use case: Exercise 7.3.2; closure properties for stopping times.

front: Supremum and infimum of stopping times
back: Use {inf T_n<t}=union {T_n<t} and {sup T_n<=t}=intersection {T_n<=t}, then build liminf and limsup from sup/inf combinations. Use case: Exercise 7.3.3; stopping-time sequence operations.

front: Use F_S by restricting to {S <= t}
back: To prove an event-defined random time is a stopping time, intersect the event A in F_S with {S <= t} and use the definition of F_S. Use case: Exercise 7.3.4; killing a stopping time on an event.

front: Comparison events for stopping times are known at either time
back: Represent {S<T} or {S>T} on {S<t} using countable rational cuts, then repeat with S and T interchanged. Use case: Exercise 7.3.5; stopped sigma-field comparison events.

front: Stopped sigma-field at S wedge T
back: Use S wedge T <= S,T for one inclusion; for the reverse, decompose A cap {S wedge T <= t} into the union of A cap {S <= t} and A cap {T <= t}. Use case: Exercise 7.3.6; identities among stopped sigma-fields.

front: Planar Brownian level hitting gives Cauchy law
back: Condition the first coordinate on the second-coordinate hitting time, then use the hitting-time Laplace transform to get characteristic function exp(-a|u|). Use case: Exercise 7.4.1; Cauchy distribution from Brownian hitting.

front: Return-time density by mixing first-hit densities
back: Condition on B_1=y and integrate the first-hitting density from y to 0 against the N(0,1) density. Use case: Exercise 7.4.2; density of the next zero after time 1.

front: Endpoint subdensity after barrier crossing
back: Reflect paths after the first hit of level a to identify P(T_a<t, B_t in dx) below a with the free Brownian density at 2a-x. Use case: Exercise 7.4.3(a,b); reflection-principle refinements.

front: Joint density of Brownian maximum and endpoint
back: Use {M_t>a}={T_a<t}, write the reflected endpoint subdensity, and differentiate the tail probability in the maximum level a. Use case: Exercise 7.4.3(c); Brownian maximum-endpoint joint law.

front: Zero-in-interval probability by hitting-time density integral
back: Condition on B_s, integrate the first-hit density over [0,t-s], swap integrals, and use u=sqrt(r/s) to get an arccos formula. Use case: Exercise 7.4.4; zero-set interval probabilities.

front: Cosh martingale for symmetric interval exit Laplace transforms
back: Average the plus and minus exponential Brownian martingales to get e^{-lambda t} cosh(sqrt(2lambda) B_t), then stop at +/-a. Use case: Exercise 7.5.1; symmetric two-sided exit transform.

front: Drifted Brownian hitting via exponential martingale
back: Choose theta solving theta^2/2 - b theta = lambda, stop exp(theta B_t - theta^2 t/2) at the linear boundary hitting time, and let the cutoff go to infinity. Use case: Exercise 7.5.2; Brownian motion with drift hitting a level.

front: Interval exit Laplace transforms from strong Markov linear equations
back: Decompose one-sided hitting transforms by whether the interval exit occurs at a or b, then solve the two equations using exp(-sqrt(2lambda) distance). Use case: Exercise 7.5.3; killed Brownian exit transforms.

front: Exit-moment comparison using Cauchy-Schwarz
back: Stop the fourth-degree Brownian polynomial martingale, then bound E(T B_T^2) by sqrt(E T^2) sqrt(E B_T^4). Use case: Exercise 7.5.4; nonsymmetric interval exit moments.

front: Sixth-degree heat polynomial for third exit-time moment
back: Solve u_t+u_xx/2=0 for x^6-c1 t x^4+c2 t^2 x^2-c3 t^3, stop at +/-a, and insert lower exit moments. Use case: Exercise 7.5.5; higher moments of symmetric Brownian exit times.

front: Exponential-square martingale plus maximal inequality
back: Use (1+t)^(-1/2) exp(B_t^2/(2(1+t))) as a positive martingale, apply Doob over exponential time blocks, and sum probabilities. Use case: Exercise 7.5.6; almost-sure Brownian growth upper bounds.

front: Ito drift test for f(B_t) martingales
back: Ito's formula shows f(B_t) has drift (1/2)f''(B_t)dt; martingality forces the finite-variation drift to vanish, hence f''=0. Use case: Exercise 7.6.1; characterizing functions of Brownian motion that are martingales.

front: Cubic Brownian martingales from Ito formula
back: Use u(t,x)=x^3-3tx for a time-space martingale, or apply Ito to x^3 and subtract the drift integral. Use case: Exercise 7.6.2; polynomial Brownian martingales.

front: Even Brownian moments by Ito recursion
back: Apply Ito to x^{2k}, take expectations, and solve beta_{2k}'(t)=k(2k-1) beta_{2k-2}(t) with beta_0=1. Use case: Exercise 7.6.3; deriving Gaussian even moments.

front: Radial Brownian Ito formula and Bessel drift
back: Approximate |x| near 0, use gradient x/|x| and Laplacian (d-1)/|x| away from 0, and identify the radial martingale part. Use case: Exercise 7.6.4; radial Brownian motion and Bessel processes.

front: Squared Brownian norm has dimension drift
back: Apply multidimensional Ito to |x|^2; the martingale part is 2 sum_i int B_s^i dB_s^i and the drift is d t. Use case: Exercise 7.6.5; computing E|B_t|^2 in R^d.

front: Conditional exit-time moment transfer
back: When a Skorokhod embedding is a mixture of Brownian exits from intervals, apply exit-time estimates conditionally on the random interval and then integrate over the mixing law. Use case: Exercise 8.1.1; transferring Exercise 7.5.4 bounds to the random time T_{U,V}.

front: Random-walk range via Donsker
back: For one-dimensional nearest-neighbor walks, visited sites form the interval between running min and max; divide by sqrt(n), apply Donsker to the continuous range functional, and drop the 1/sqrt(n) endpoint correction. Use case: Exercise 8.1.2 and random-walk range asymptotics.

front: Head-run CLT variance from 1-dependence
back: For indicators of the pattern HT in fair coin flips, center by mu=1/4 and compute long-run variance as Var(eta_0)+2 Cov(eta_0,eta_1)=1/16 because adjacent HT events are mutually exclusive. Use case: Exercise 8.3.1; finite-dependence stationary CLT computations.

front: Computing Gordin's Y0 in finite-dependence examples
back: Use Y0=sum_j>=0(E(X_j|F_0)-E(X_j|F_{-1})); finite dependence kills all but finitely many terms, leaving explicit conditional expectations. Use case: Exercise 8.3.1 and martingale approximation examples.

front: One-sided Brownian bridge tail by conditioned reflection
back: Compute P(max bridge > b) by viewing the bridge as Brownian motion conditioned on B_1=0 and using the reflected Brownian density on {T_b<1}. Use case: Exercise 8.4.1; bridge extrema and Kolmogorov-Smirnov limit calculations.

front: Brownian bridge from time-changed Brownian motion
back: To prove X_t=(1-t)B(t/(1-t)) is a bridge, show it is centered Gaussian with covariance s(1-t) for s<=t and continuous extension X_1=0. Use case: Exercise 8.4.2; recognizing Gaussian process identities.

front: Brownian bridge Markov kernel by density cancellation
back: Write pinned finite-dimensional densities as products of Brownian transition densities ending at zero; divide joint densities so all pre-s factors cancel, leaving a kernel depending only on the present state. Use case: Exercise 8.4.3; proving Markov property for conditioned Gaussian processes.

front: Bridge divided by remaining time is a martingale
back: Use E(B_t^0|B_s^0)=((1-t)/(1-s))B_s^0 to show B_t^0/(1-t) is a martingale; L2 norms grow like t/(1-t), so it is not L2 bounded. Use case: Exercise 8.4.4; bridge martingales and terminal singularity checks.

front: Heavy-tailed jumps obstruct LIL scaling
back: If E|X|^alpha is infinite with alpha<2, tail sums at scale n^{1/alpha} diverge; Borel-Cantelli gives infinitely many huge increments, forcing adjacent partial sums above sqrt(n log log n) scale. Use case: Exercise 8.5.1 and necessity intuition for finite variance in LIL.

front: Scalar LIL limit set from Strassen endpoint evaluation
back: Apply the continuous map f -> f(1) to Strassen's compact LIL limit set; Cauchy-Schwarz bounds endpoints by [-1,1], and linear functions f(t)=at realize every endpoint. Use case: Exercise 8.5.2; deriving scalar limsup/limit-set results from functional LIL.

front: Backward heat martingale template (proof-template)
back: For u_t=(1/2)Delta u, apply Ito to u(t-s,B_s); the time derivative cancels the Brownian drift and leaves a local martingale. Use case: Section 9.2 martingale checks and any backward parabolic uniqueness argument.

front: Bounded heat solution uniqueness by terminal martingale (proof-template)
back: Boundedness upgrades the backward local martingale to a uniformly integrable martingale; sending s up to t identifies u(t,x) with E_x f(B_t). Use case: Heat equation uniqueness and bounded parabolic Cauchy problems.

front: Gaussian approximate identity at t=0 (calculation-template)
back: Write B_t as sqrt(t)Z; bounded convergence proves E f(x_n+sqrt(t_n)Z) tends to f(x) when f is bounded continuous. Use case: Initial-condition verification for heat and Feynman-Kac formulas.

front: Heat-kernel derivative majorants (regularity-template)
back: On t bounded away from zero, derivatives of the Gaussian kernel are polynomial times a Gaussian, giving integrable majorants for differentiating under the integral. Use case: Proving C^{1,2} smoothing for heat semigroups.

front: Source-term martingale for inhomogeneous heat equations (proof-template)
back: For u_t=(1/2)Delta u+g, add integral_0^s g(B_r)dr to u(t-s,B_s) so the drift cancels. Use case: Duhamel representation and source-term uniqueness.

front: Duhamel uniqueness from bounded martingales (proof-template)
back: A bounded inhomogeneous heat solution with zero initial data equals E_x integral_0^t g(B_s)ds by stopping the source-term martingale at s=t. Use case: Identifying source-forced heat solutions and occupation integrals.

front: Short-time Markov-property PDE verification (calculation-template)
back: Use v(t+h,x)=E_x[v(t,B_h)+short-time reward], Taylor expand v(t,B_h), divide by h, and let h down to zero. Use case: Showing smooth Brownian representations solve heat, Poisson, and Feynman-Kac PDEs.

front: Holder cancellation for singular heat-source kernels (regularity-template)
back: Subtract g(x) inside P_s g(x)-g(x); Holder continuity gives O(s^{alpha/2}), offsetting the small-time singularity. Use case: Regularity of inhomogeneous heat and potential-kernel formulas.

front: Exponential potential weight martingale (proof-template)
back: Multiply by exp(integral c(B_s)ds); the finite-variation product rule adds exactly the c u drift needed for cancellation. Use case: Feynman-Kac and Schrodinger martingale derivations.

front: Bounded Feynman-Kac uniqueness (proof-template)
back: If c and u are bounded on finite time intervals, the weighted martingale is bounded; stopping at t gives u(t,x)=E_x[f(B_t)exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds)]. Use case: Parabolic PDEs with bounded multiplicative potentials.

front: Smooth Feynman-Kac short-time check (calculation-template)
back: Use v(t+h,x)=E_x[e^{int_0^h c(B_s)ds}v(t,B_h)], then expand the Brownian move and the exponential to get v_t=(1/2)Delta v+c v. Use case: Verifying smooth Feynman-Kac representations solve the PDE.

front: Feynman-Kac smoothness by Duhamel reduction (regularity-template)
back: Use e^{A_t}-1=integral_0^t c(B_s)e^{A_t-A_s}ds to decompose v into a heat solution plus an inhomogeneous heat solution with source c v. Use case: Regularity proofs for Feynman-Kac formulas with Holder f and c.

front: Liouville theorem via heat semigroup flattening (proof-template)
back: A bounded harmonic h satisfies h(x)=E h(x+B_t); as t grows, two Gaussian laws with fixed mean separation have vanishing total variation distance, so h(x)=h(y). Use case: Bounded harmonic functions on all of R^d.

front: Recurrent Brownian motion forces nonnegative superharmonic functions constant (proof-template)
back: Nonnegative superharmonic functions yield supermartingales; in d=1,2 Brownian motion hits every small neighborhood, so optional stopping gives f(x)>=f(y) and symmetry gives equality. Use case: Superharmonic Liouville-type exercises in recurrent dimensions.

front: Radial superharmonic examples in high dimension (example-pattern)
back: Use the radial Laplacian g''(r)+(d-1)g'(r)/r; functions such as (1+r^2)^{-(d-2)/2} are smooth nonconstant nonnegative superharmonic for d>=3. Use case: Constructing counterexamples to recurrent-dimensional constancy in transient dimensions.

front: Escape probability is the nonuniqueness direction (proof-template)
back: When Brownian motion can avoid exiting G, q(x)=P_x(tau=infinity) is a bounded zero-boundary harmonic function; all bounded zero-boundary solutions differ by c q. Use case: Dirichlet problems in domains where exit is not almost sure.

front: Flat cone boundary regularity (proof-template)
back: Use zeros of the perpendicular Brownian coordinate and the induced Cauchy trace on the hyperplane to show Brownian motion immediately hits a flat exterior cone. Use case: Checking regular boundary points under weaker geometric exterior conditions.

front: Infinite ball occupation in recurrent dimensions (proof-template)
back: Use recurrence to get infinitely many returns to a ball and the strong Markov property to sum positive occupation increments. Use case: Occupation-time dichotomy for Brownian motion in d<=2.

front: Gamma change of variables for transient potential kernels (calculation-template)
back: For d>=3, integrate the heat kernel over time and use s=|x-y|^2/(2t) to get Gamma(d/2-1)/(2 pi^{d/2}) |x-y|^{2-d}. Use case: Green-function and expected occupation-density calculations.

front: Modified potential kernels in recurrent dimensions (calculation-template)
back: Subtract a reference heat kernel before integrating over time; in d=1 this gives -|x-y| and in d=2 gives -(1/pi)log|x-y|. Use case: Using potential kernels when the full-space occupation integral diverges.

front: Frullani integral for the two-dimensional log kernel (calculation-template)
back: After u=1/(2t), the difference of two planar heat kernels becomes integral_0^infty (e^{-a^2u}-e^{-u})du/u=-2log a. Use case: Deriving the d=2 logarithmic potential kernel.

front: Distributional delta normalization of Green kernels (proof-template)
back: Integrate by parts outside a small ball and let the radius shrink; the boundary flux fixes the constant so (1/2)Delta G=-delta_0. Use case: Checking Green-function constants and fundamental solutions.

front: Exit-time moment recursion (proof-template)
back: For m_n(x)=E_x tau^n, the Markov property or Dynkin formula gives (1/2)Delta m_n=-n m_{n-1} with zero boundary values. Use case: Computing higher moments of Brownian exit times.

front: Second exit-time moment Poisson system (calculation-template)
back: First solve (1/2)Delta v=-1 for v=E tau; then solve (1/2)Delta w=-2v for w=E tau^2, both with zero boundary values. Use case: Exercise 9.7.1 and second-moment exit-time computations.

front: Schrodinger weighted martingale (proof-template)
back: For (1/2)Delta u+c u=0, Ito plus the finite-variation product rule makes u(B_t)exp(integral_0^t c(B_s)ds) a local martingale before exit. Use case: Boundary Feynman-Kac formulas for elliptic equations with potential.

front: Gauge integrability condition (criterion)
back: Before applying boundary Feynman-Kac with positive potential, check w(x)=E_x exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds); bounded c alone does not ensure finiteness. Use case: Avoiding false Schrodinger Feynman-Kac representations.

front: Interval eigenvalue threshold for exponential exit times (calculation-template)
back: For tau exiting (-a,a), solve (1/2)u''+gamma u=0 with boundary value one; finiteness holds exactly below gamma=pi^2/(8a^2). Use case: Explicit exponential exit-time moments and gauge blow-up examples.

front: Finite-gauge boundary Feynman-Kac uniqueness (proof-template)
back: Finite bounded gauge controls the terminal exponential weight and kills the tail term, giving u(x)=E_x[f(B_tau)exp(integral_0^tau c(B_s)ds)]. Use case: Uniqueness of bounded Schrodinger Dirichlet solutions.

front: Local exit expansion for smooth Schrodinger representations (calculation-template)
back: Stop at a small ball, expand v(B_sigma) and exp(integral_0^sigma c(B_s)ds), then divide by E sigma to get (1/2)Delta v+c v=0. Use case: Verifying smooth Schrodinger Feynman-Kac formulas solve the PDE.
